title: Electrical tunability of terahertz nonlinearity in graphene
authors: Sergey Kovalev, Hassan A. Hafez, Klaas-Jan Tielrooij, Jan-Christoph Deinert, Igor Ilyakov, Nilesh Awari, David Alcaraz, Karuppasamy Soundarapandian, David Saleta, Semyon Germanskiy, Min Chen, Mohammed Bawatna, Bertram Green, Frank H. L. Koppens, Martin Mittendorff, Mischa Bonn, Michael Gensch, Dmitry Turchinovich
abstract: Graphene is conceivably the most nonlinear optoelectronic material. Its nonlinear optical coefficients in the terahertz (THz) frequency range surpass those of other materials by many orders of magnitude. This, in particular, allows one to use graphene for extremely efficient up-conversion of sub-THz electronic input signals into the THz frequency range at room temperature and under ambient conditions, thus paving the way for practical graphene-based ultrahigh-frequency electronic technology. Here, we show that the THz nonlinearity of graphene can be efficiently controlled using electrical gating, with gating voltages as low as a few volts. For example, optimal electrical gating enhances the power conversion efficiency in THz third-harmonic generation in graphene by about two orders of magnitude. This essentially converts graphene from an almost perfectly linear, inert electronic material to a material with the highest possible THz nonlinearity. We demonstrate gating control of THz nonlinearity of graphene for both ultrashort single-cycle and quasi-monochromatic multi-cycle input signals. Our experimental results are in quantitative agreement with a physical model of graphene nonlinearity, describing the time-dependent thermodynamic balance maintained within the electronic population of graphene during interaction with ultrafast electric fields. Our results can serve as a basis for straightforward and accurate design of devices and applications for efficient electronic signal processing in graphene at ultra-high frequencies.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the massive nonlinear optical response of graphene in the terahertz regime can be electrically tuned using very low voltages. By adjusting the Fermi energy through gating, the researchers achieved a 70-fold modulation in THz transmission and a 100-fold increase in third-harmonic generation efficiency. The mechanism is driven by the ultrafast heating and subsequent cooling of the electronic population. This work provides a foundation for developing highly efficient, voltage-controlled graphene-based components for ultra-high-frequency signal processing.
main_problem: Achieving efficient, electrical control over the large nonlinear terahertz (THz) response in graphene for applications in high-frequency electronics.
main_result: Graphene's THz nonlinearity can be tuned by a factor of ~70 using low gating voltages (a few volts), with optimal gating enhancing third-harmonic generation efficiency by approximately two orders of magnitude.
method: The study uses nonlinear THz spectroscopy, including THz pump-probe and multi-cycle THz time-domain spectroscopy, combined with a thermodynamic model of carrier heating and cooling.
model_or_system: The system is CVD-grown monolayer graphene on a quartz substrate, controlled via a polymer electrolyte gate. The physical model is a single-band thermodynamic approach describing the interplay of THz-induced electronic heating and phonon-mediated cooling.
key_observables: THz third-harmonic generation (THG) efficiency, THz saturable absorption, differential transmission (delta T/T0), and carrier temperature (Te).
important_parameters: Fermi energy (E_F) between 50 and 200 meV, peak electric field strengths up to 80 kV/cm, and momentum scattering time (tau) dominated by long-range Coulomb scattering.
main_assumptions: The single-band model assumes carrier density conservation and is valid when E_F > k_B Te; the modulation speed is currently limited by the polymer electrolyte gate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows THz waveforms and the temporal modulation of conductivity/absorbance; Figure 3 illustrates harmonic electric field enhancement; Supplementary figures show conductivity spectra, electronic temperature evolution, and Hall measurement validation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of THz nonlinearity in graphene, describes the experimental setup and gating mechanism, presents results for both single-cycle and multi-cycle pulses, validates the findings with a thermodynamic model, and concludes with implications for high-frequency device design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
